text: The surgeon places ports in the left upper quadrant, aligning them in a straight line spaced eight centimeters apart, extending towards the right lower quadrant.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to switch the transverse colon and right colon to the abdominal wall, enhancing exposure and freeing the instruments for further dissection.

The surgeon opens the mesenteric envelope and dissects tumors using a medial-lateral approach, exposing the duodenum. The ileocolic vessels are identified, with the ileocolic vein clipped and divided from the superior mesenteric vein. The distal ileum is identified, and the mesentery is divided to address pelvic adhesions, freeing the small intestine for later anastomosis.

The surgeon uses the robotic fourth arm to continue the cephalad dissection, identifying and clipping the right colic vein and artery. The robotic clips are applied to divide these vessels without laparoscopic assistance.
The surgeon dissects over the pancreas and duodenum, demonstrating proximity to these structures. The suture is taken down for further retraction using the robotic fourth arm.
The surgeon identifies the right branch of the midcolic vessels, clips them, and then divides them using robotic instruments.

The surgical team replaces a sponge underneath the transverse colon and pulls it caudally to enter the lesser sac. They divide the omentum and identify the right gastroepiploic vessels on the greater curvature of the stomach, which are then divided. The dissection proceeds towards the hepatic flexure, connecting spaces around the gastrocolic trunk, and continues to mobilize the right colon using a previously taken down suture to relieve tension.
The surgeon divides the distal ileum and marks it with a suture for later reference. The transverse colon is also divided, and any remaining attachments are completely severed using a vessel sealer.

The surgeon has completed the disconnection and placed the specimen aside. The distal ileum is positioned close to the transverse colon. The anastomosis is parked in the right upper quadrant, with a suture placed and the fourth arm used for proper exposure.
The surgeon performs an isoperistaltic anastomosis, creating a metacollotomy to connect the bowel segments.

The surgeon uses ICG to assess the blood supply of the small intestine and transverse colon. A 45 stapler is then used for the anastomosis, with the fourth arm aligning the anastomosis. The common channel opening is closed using a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture in a canal suture technique.
The completed anastomosis is visible, with the barbed suture locked in place. The surgeon uses ICG to assess the blood supply to the anastomosis.
